forecaster_note: Surfline (Mon 20 Jul): "Great looking day — head to overhead and clean options." The E swell mix is still delivering ~6.9ft @ 11s from 72° — right in your 30–80° sweet spot — with a smaller S line (3.6ft @ 12s, 177°) underneath. Energy peaks around 740 kJ. Morning offshores (W 281°) have it clean and organised early; wind swings N through mid-morning and onto a light NE onshore by mid-afternoon, so the early session is the good one. Water 17.2°C, air 10°C at dawn. — Surfline Sydney forecast team

wind.summary: Light W offshore 8 kph at dawn, holding clean until about 11am — then it tips N and onto a light NE onshore (14 kph) by 3pm. Surf before midday.

shark.summary: 2.33m White Shark tagged and released from a SMART drumline at North Curl Curl, 9:34am Sun 19 Jul. Also a shark observed by SLS drone at Whale Beach 3:46pm Sun 19 Jul (water evacuated) — well north of your spots. Nothing new logged overnight for Dee Why, Long Reef or Manly.

days.label: Today
days.note: Best window of the week and it's a Monday — light W offshore over a 6.9ft/11s E swell from 72°, dead in your favoured 30–80° band, with ~740 kJ behind it. Clean and organised from first light until roughly 11am, then the wind goes N and onto a NE onshore by mid-afternoon. Dee Why Point and Long Reef are the picks; Curl Curl carries a fresh shark alert.

dee-why.window: Dawn to about 11am while the W offshore holds. Low tide 6:26am means the banks are shallow and punchy first up; the push toward the 12:50pm high fattens it but keeps the Point rights running longer. Wind flips N around midday and NE by 3pm — after that it's over.

tip.label: Frontside tip
tip.text: 72° is exactly the angle the Point wants — no S wrap problem today. Sit wide on the rights and let the 11s period do the work; with 740 kJ behind it the sets will stand up properly rather than fatten out.

long-reef.window: Biggest of the four — the reef takes the full E energy at 4–5ft and the morning offshore lets it show shape. Best from sunrise to about 10:30am; the incoming tide adds push. Step-up board territory.

tip.label: Frontside tip
tip.text: Solid rights off the reef with real weight — commit early on the drop and use the offshore to hold your line. If it feels a size up on Dee Why, that's because it is.

alerts: 🦈 Shark alert — 2.33m White Shark tagged on a SMART drumline at North Curl Curl, 9:34am Sun 19 Jul. Check SharkSmart before paddling out.
curl-curl.window: Clean shifting peaks under the morning offshore, chest to overhead. Same early window as the rest — in by 7, out by 11. Note the drumline alert at the north end from yesterday morning.

tip.label: Backside tip
tip.text: The E angle makes the lefts here run better than usual — pick the peak with the sand behind it and drive off the bottom early on your backhand.

manly.window: North Steyne is holding good size for once at 2–4ft, clean under the W offshore. Slightly more wind-tolerant than the open beaches when the NE arrives, so it's the fallback if you can't get out before 11am.

tip.label: Backside tip
tip.text: Straight E swell means the banks along North Steyne all switch on — walk until you find one peeling rather than settling for the first close-out.

days.label: Tomorrow
days.note: Swell eases hard — energy roughly halves to ~350 kJ as the E pulse (5.3ft @ 10s, 70°) drops away. Still clean early under a NW-to-N offshore with a decent morning session on offer, but smaller and softer than today. Long Reef keeps the most size.

dee-why.window: Light NW offshore at dawn going N by 9am — clean but noticeably weaker than Monday. Fun waist-to-head on the Point if you can get out first thing.

long-reef.window: Holds the most residual energy again — chest to overhead off the reef, cleanest before 9am. The pick if you want size on a fading swell.

curl-curl.window: Tidy chest-to-head peaks early before the NE gets into it around midday. Easy, high-wave-count session.

manly.window: Smallest of the four as the E swell backs off — clean but modest. Longboard or fuller-volume shortboard weather.

days.label: Weekend
days.note: A big S groundswell arrives — energy jumps to 1240 kJ Saturday and 1380 kJ Sunday, the most raw power of the whole run. But the direction is the problem: 193° Sat and 177° Sun is effectively dead south, which wraps badly into Dee Why and gets largely blocked at Manly. Long Reef and Curl Curl are the S-exposed picks. Saturday morning also has a stiff WSW offshore (23 kph gusting 51) — clean but hard to sit in; Sunday morning is the gentler, better-groomed of the two.

alerts: ⚠️ Dead-S swell (177–193°) — big energy on paper but it wraps poorly into Dee Why. Expect far less size than the kJ suggests.
💨 Sat morning WSW gusting to 51 kph — clean but hard work.
dee-why.window: Sunday morning is the better of the two: lighter W offshore and the S swell has more size behind it. Don't be fooled by the energy number — the angle kills most of it here.

alerts: 💨 Sat morning WSW gusting to 51 kph — expect chop on the faces despite being offshore.
long-reef.window: Best-exposed to the S swell and holding real size both days — head to well overhead. Sunday 8–11am under a lighter W offshore is the session to plan for. Step-up board.

curl-curl.window: Takes the S swell better than Dee Why — solid chest-to-overhead peaks with the offshore holding them open. Sunday morning cleanest.

alerts: ⚠️ S swell is largely shadowed by the southern headland — smallest of the four despite the big energy figure.
manly.window: Clean but shadowed. Only worth it Sunday if the bigger spots are too much, or you want an easy paddle.
